Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want it simple—steady money coming in and not a bunch of complicated stuff.
Advisor: That makes sense. Why don’t we start high-level: across all sources, what’s your household gross income for the year?
Client 2: All in, our household gross income is 61750 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do your total monthly expenses run, just for the household?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—total monthly expenses are 2650.

Client 2: All in, our household gross income is 61750 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do your total monthly expenses run, just for the household?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—total monthly expenses are 2650.
Advisor: Okay. And any monthly debt payments—mortgage, car loans, credit cards—anything like that?
Client 1: No, monthly debt payments are 0. We don’t have loans outstanding.

Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want it simple—steady money coming in and not a bunch of complicated stuff.

Client 2: We track it pretty closely—total monthly expenses are 2650.

Client 1: No, monthly debt payments are 0. We don’t have loans outstanding.
Advisor: Great. Let’s shift to assets—roughly what you’d consider investable, so brokerage, retirement accounts, cash accounts—what’s the total you’re working with?
Client 1: Investable assets total 516650.
Advisor: Perfect. Now I’m going to break that into the buckets you already have, just so I’m not missing anything. First—do you have a joint taxable brokerage account?
Client 1: Yes, we have a joint taxable brokerage account, and it’s 237100.

Client 1: Yes, I’ve got a private markets account on an advisor platform, and it’s 36550.
Advisor: Okay. And then your home—your primary residence—what do you estimate that’s worth today?
Client 2: The primary residence is 276000.
Advisor: Great, thank you. Now, income details—Linda, you mentioned wanting steady cash flow. What are the main income streams coming in?
Client 2: I have rental income that comes weekly, and the annual amount is 16450.

Client 2: I always get mixed up—like, is that the refund thing or the percentage?
Advisor: Totally fair—just the percentage bracket, the marginal rate. If you’ve seen it on your return or your accountant has told you, what’s the bracket band you’re usually in?
Client 1: It’s 37%.
Advisor: Okay, thank you—hang on, I’m just noting that down. And we’ll dig into what’s driving that—your income streams, required distributions, all that—next time.
Client 2: Yes, please. I just don’t want anything complicated.

Advisor: Hi David, hi Linda—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m David. We’re both retired, and I want a clearer plan—income, taxes, and making sure Linda’s set if something happens to me.
Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want it simple—steady money coming in and not a bunch of complicated stuff.
Advisor: That makes sense. Why don’t we start high-level: across all sources, what’s your household gross income for the year?
Client 2: All in, our household gross income is 61750 a year.

Advisor: Okay—thanks, David and Linda. Before we wrap, I want to make sure the debt side is truly clean. When you say “no loans,” does that include the mortgage—meaning you don’t owe anything on the house?
Client 1: Yes, that includes the mortgage. The mortgage balance outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Got it. And besides a mortgage, any other debts at all—car loan, credit cards you carry month to month, personal loans?
Client 2: No, we don’t carry anything like that. Non-mortgage debt outstanding is 0.
Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m consistent—total loans outstanding across everything would be… zero as well?

Advisor: Hi David, hi Linda—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into numbers, what prompted you to reach out now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m David. We’re both retired, and I want a clearer plan—income, taxes, and making sure Linda’s set if something happens to me.
Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want it simple—steady money coming in and not a bunch of complicated stuff.
Advisor: That makes sense. Why don’t we start high-level: across all sources, what’s your household gross income for the year?
Client 2: All in, our household gross income is 61750 a year.

Advisor: Great. Next one is more about how we work together. Our firm is an RIA—registered investment advisor. Is that the advisory relationship you’re looking for, versus, say, a brokerage-only setup?
Client 1: Yes, that’s what we want—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And last one—risk comfort. When markets swing, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client 2: I’d say moderate. I don’t want anything too fancy.
Advisor: Moderate—okay. David, does that match how you think about it too?

Client 2: And I’m Linda. I just want it simple—steady money coming in and not a bunch of complicated stuff.
Advisor: That makes sense. Why don’t we start high-level: across all sources, what’s your household gross income for the year?
Client 2: All in, our household gross income is 61750 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what do your total monthly expenses run, just for the household?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—total monthly expenses are 2650.

Advisor: Okay. When you say none, that’s like no one relying on you for regular support—just the two of you.
Client 2: Right, just us.
Advisor: Great. Next one is more about how we work together. Our firm is an RIA—registered investment advisor. Is that the advisory relationship you’re looking for, versus, say, a brokerage-only setup?
Client 1: Yes, that’s what we want—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Okay, thank you. And last one—risk comfort. When markets swing, do you see yourselves as conservative, moderate, or aggressive?

Client 2: Okay, we can do that.
Advisor: Perfect. Two quick household basics and then I’ll let you go—promise.
Advisor: David and Linda, just confirming your marital status for planning—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it, thanks. And dependents—anyone financially dependent on you right now? Kids you still support, anyone you claim, that kind of thing.

Client 2: I do the day-to-day bills, but yes—David’s the primary on the accounts.
Advisor: Got it. And then income for you personally, David—what’s your gross annual income these days? Even if it’s mostly from retirement sources.
Client 1: It’s 42800 gross for the year.
Advisor: Thank you—hang on, I’m just noting that. And just to make sure I’m describing it right, your occupation group would be retired as well, correct?
Client 1: Correct—retired.
